question: Explain why a Pearson correlation coefficient of 0.8 does not necessarily imply that one variable causes the other.
expectedPoints: Correlation measures the strength and direction of a linear relationship, not causation.
A high correlation could be due to a third, confounding variable influencing both.
The relationship might be coincidental or a result of the specific data sample.

question: Describe the process of designing an experiment to test if a new algorithm (Algorithm A) sorts data faster than the standard method (Algorithm B). Your answer should cover the key steps from the initial question to the statistical test.
expectedPoints: Define the null hypothesis (H0: no difference in mean sorting time) and alternative hypothesis (H1: a significant difference exists).
Identify and control for confounding variables (e.g., input data size, data type, hardware specifications).
Outline the data collection: run both algorithms on multiple, randomized data sets and record the time.
State that an independent two-sample t-test or a one-way ANOVA (if more than two algorithms are compared) would be the appropriate statistical test to analyze the results.

question: A computer scientist runs a one-way ANOVA to compare the performance of three different compression algorithms across 30 trials each. The resulting ANOVA table shows a p-value of 0.03. Interpret this result in the context of the experiment. Subsequently, the scientist calculates a Pearson correlation of -0.92 between compression ratio and processing time for one of the algorithms. Interpret this correlation and discuss its limitations for making broader conclusions.
expectedPoints: The ANOVA p-value of 0.03 (typically < 0.05) leads to rejection of the null hypothesis; it indicates a statistically significant difference in the mean performance between at least two of the three compression algorithms.

The Pearson correlation of -0.92 indicates a very strong negative linear relationship for that specific algorithm; as compression ratio increases (more compression), the processing time decreases (or vice versa).

A key limitation is that this correlation only describes the relationship for that single algorithm tested; it cannot be generalized to the other algorithms or different types of compression techniques without further study.
The correlation is also limited to the linear component of the relationship and does not imply causation between ratio and time.
